content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Need parody movies to crack me up

**Score:** 41
**Body:** Hi, I used to watch funny parody movies. I love all of the \[A Scary Movie\] movies, \[A superhero movie\] and \[Epic movie\].I tried to find a few more like those such as ' Not another teen movie' and 'disaster movie' but could not like them, they were not as funny as those. Can y'all suggest me a few ? thank you.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This Is Spinal Tap!
2. Young Frankenstein
3. Airplane!
4. Top Secret! (from the same crew that made Airplane!) is a particular favorite of mine.
5. Don't be a Menace to South Central while Drinking your Juice in the Hood
6. Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping
7. The Naked Gun, Blazing Saddles and Airplane!

\*Edit: The Naked Gun from 1988. Haven't seen the remake yet.
8. Johnny Dangerously
9. Best In Show
10. Robin Hood: Men in Tights (Best if you've seen the Kevin Costner version.) pretty much anything Mel Brooks, really!

## 2. One Room Movies

**Score:** 44
**Body:** I love One Room Movies - in any genre, where people get together, don’t really leave the room and throughout their conversations their relationships change, people change or some revelations are made. 

Like when a bunch of friends are having a dinner party, and somebody mentioned something insignificant, which is then questioned by somebody else and step by step, word by word a huge secret is unravelled, or some masks slip etc. 

The best example I can think of is French movie “Nothing to Hide”, where a bunch of old friends get together and as a joke they agree to show any message on their phones to everyone at the table. Starting harmless enough the story takes a few turns, without them even leaving the apartment.

Another example would be a classic movie like “The Breakfast Club“, where people who don’t normally interact much, are stuck in the same space with nowhere to go. And see each other differently afterwards.

Eight Women would be another good one.

Also when people are stuck on a long flight, or in a broken elevator etc. 

Basically, not much movements or physical actions, but rather interactions, psychology, character developments, unexpected revelations etc., like a theatre play  



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 12 Angry Men
2. The hateful eight I think is mostly one room. 

The man from earth is probably the most fantastic so like I can think of.
3. If you’re into horror, the original Saw movie. It’s mostly in that one room.
4. Rear Window
  - Rope
5. All on the darker/trippier side:

Eraserhead (1977) - no need to explain

Vivarium (2019) - two people stuck in a house over several months/years

Coherence (2013) - dinner party gone wrong
6. Man from earth
7. Buried. But it's not a room... It's a coffin.

Carnage: it's polankski so your morals may preclude this one but following a incident of school yard violence the parents get together for a civil meal. Cracks begin to show almost instantly. Based on a stage play it matches your description to a tee.
8. Deathtrap (1982)
9. Locke (2013) is pretty much set entirely in one car with only Tom Hardy on screen (he makes phone calls throughout the film). I really enjoyed it, definitely recommended.

## 3. Weird, female centric horror

**Score:** 38
**Body:** I’m looking for less conventional horror movies, not slashers or something that relies on jump scares to scare you. It doesn’t have to be scary at all actually, I’m more so looking for an unsettling, eerie or uncomfortable vibe. Preferably female main character/cast. (Bonus points if it was actually directed or written by a woman). I don’t mind gore or body horror.

I’m thinking movies like: Raw (2016), Saint Maud (2019), Black Swan (2010), The Substance (2024), Twin Peaks: Fire, Walk with Me (1992), Eyes Without a Face (1960).

Edit: Thank you for all the great recommendations! I have quite the list to get through now. :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ginger Snaps and Jennifer’s Body both don’t get the attention they deserve. Funny and very feminist horror

Also Teeth, for a little more weird (but still funny)

And a classic with that more ethereal, unreal vibe, In the Company of Wolves
  - Ginger Snaps was so good.  Also good bit of Canadiana.
2. Midsommar seems to fit this description well
3. Under the Skin
4. Midsommer
5. Possession, one of my favourite movies of all time 
6. Annihilation
7. Men , 2022
8. american Mary
9. If you've seen Raw, Titane is next. 

Humanist Vampire Seeking Consenting Suicidal Person is weird, not particularly scary though.

## 4. Comfort movies to watch

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Please recommend movies that may not necessarily be good to watch but something that is just silly or lighthearted or just dumb fun, like the daddy’s home movies or the other guys. Something I could turn on on my phone and fall asleep to.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chef (2014) is a good one. Definitely a feel good movie.
2. Not quite the vibe you're describing but for me My Neighbor Totoro is a comfort movie for sure
  - I love that movie. My daughter was like 5 through 14 when I used to watch it a lot. I just remember the grandma always saying "May?!"
3. death at a funeral

my cousin vinny

big lebowski
4. Chef (2014) is my ultimate comfort movie. I watch it 4-6 times a year 😂
5. The Change-Up 

Silver Streak 

Larry Crowne 

Cousins 

Mumford 

The Intern 

That Thing You Do 

Almost Famous
6. The Princess Bride was mine as a kid. So many winter nights by the fireplace watching a taped broadcast, falling asleep on the couch. It's such a gentle and charming love story.

But recently, Black Dynamite. Goofy but smarter than your average parody, and made as a loving pastiche rather than a venomous skewering. It's only 70 minutes or so too, so they pack a lot of plot into it. Perfect for falling asleep to, which I have.
7. Daddy Daycare
8. The Burbs
9. Rat Race (2001)

## 5. Movie that will grab my attention quickly and not let go

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I just finished active cancer treatment and I have found that my attention span has been shortened.   I've tried watching several  movies and TV series but end up bailing out, even highly regarded films.  I just watched the Swedish version of the girl with the dragon tattoo from start to finish.  The BBC show Blue Lights was good.   **what other movies etc will grab my attention and keep me interested??***  Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Snatch (2000) crime comedy.
2. Good time
3. Run Lola Run

Trainspottting

Fight Club
4. I too must be got right away.  Weapons did it for me.
5. Nocturnal Animals, Psycho (most Hitchcock films actually), Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind. The X/Pearl/Maxxxine trilogy. Those are ones I can think of off the top of my head that had me from jump.

There’s an excellent limited series called The English which is immediately gripping. For TV shows: Severance, The Americans, Fargo, and Bates Motel.
6. Gone Girl
7. No Country for Old Men
8. Not a movie recommendation but one thing that might help with keeping you focused is watching foreign films with subtitles, like you did with the girl with the dragon tattoo (I assume, maybe you speak swedish) For me the act of having to read to understand what's happening forces me to focus on the movie.
9. All the Deadpool movies start fast and don’t stop. Action and Humor. 

Boy Kills World

Late Night with the Devil 

No One Lives

Atomic Blonde

Extraction 1 & 2

Rebel Ridge
10. If you want some anime

Redline - intergalactic car race

Tokyo Godfathers - THE Christmas movie. Takes maybe 10 minutes but then buckle up.

Mind Game - from its batshit animation style to it's bat shit story this one is a doozie

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a movie that proved you don’t need a big budget to make a high-concept film.

**Score:** 574
**Body:** Upgrade was made in a budget of Just 3 million.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cube (1997) 

Budget was an estimated $365,000CAD

Fantastic film with twists that keep you guessing and wondering what the heck is going on. 

I loved it.
2. Paranormal activity had the budget of a used Prius
3. Primer - $7k budget
  - This is not a movie, it’s a piece of art.
4. Green Room
5. Southland Tales is probably too ambitious for its own good
6. The fare 

Coherence
7. Clerks
8. The Man From Earth. 
9. The Blair Witch Project.

## 2. House of dynamite on netflix is the worst movie ive ever seen in my life

**Score:** 416
**Body:** This is seriously the worst movie ever made. It loops 3 times on the same timeline from 3 different perspectives. It focuses on how people are feeling about the situation and their emotional response to the incoming icbm. 

Do not watch this movie.

The movie ends without any info provided on who launched it, if it really ever detonates, and what the response is.

Its like telling the same story 3 times then not giving the ending.


### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fully agreed. When I realized the bomb would never go off, I let out a huge fart to give myself an ending 
  - The real treasure was the farts we made along the way
2. It’s not very good, but worst movie you’ve ever seen? You can’t have seen many movies. There have been plenty of worse movies this year alone.
  - The term "worst ever" has completely lost meaning, and people say it as if they don't sound like Comic Book Guy from The Simpsons.
3. I guess I'm literally the only one here, but I thought it was solid. Not amazing, not terrible. First third was some of the best stuff I've seen this year; rest was fine. I thought the lack of ending was good in theory but worked less well due to the rashoman style giving us the same cliffhanger multiple times.
  - Also thought it was fine, especially the beginning. 

To be a little pedantic though, Rashomon style is different perspectives that contradict each other, with unreliable narrators. This was just three different looks at the exact same events.
    - Yup, fair enough. thought about saying rashomon-ish. Meant it as shorthand for different perspectives
4. Calling *A House of Dynamite* ‘the worst movie ever made’ because it doesn’t say who launched the missile is like saying *Jaws* sucks because you don’t see the shark enough.

The non-ending *is* the point. By refusing to show who fired, whether it detonates, or what the U.S. does, the film traps you in the same anxiety as the characters. What your mind invents is scarier than any mushroom cloud. This is form matching function.

The editing and sound design are low-key brilliant. Scenes of quiet dread cut hard into noise, tension building and breaking without ever actually releasing it. It’s deliberate and artfully done.

Not a masterpiece by any means, Bigelow's *The* *Hurt Locker* is still her best movie, but it lacks the real artistry that is lacking from most of Hollywood’s recycled IP slop. And the acting’s solid, the cinematography sharp, the characters human.

If you want an actual ‘worst movie ever,’ try Netflix’s *How It Ends.* Ambiguous without any purpose behind it. Just a road trip to nowhere in an apocalypse that isn't explained properly.

People on Reddit calling so many things ‘the worst ever’ has made it lose all meaning. You literally sound like Comic Book Guy from the Simpsons.
5. You should see the War of the Worlds movie amazon released this year
6. So many people complaining about ending. But that’s real life. No one might ever know who or why.

## 3. Who is the greatest JOHN of hollywood?

**Score:** 293

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. John Williams
  - Has to be, right?

120+ movie scores. He's pretty fkng prolific!
2. How great was jonh cazale
  - Every film he starred in was Oscar nominated for best picture.
3. You didn't put John Candy on there?
4. He’s a Goodman, and thorough
5. Where is Lithgow?
6. Bit of an unpopular one maybe but I think Turturro is one of the best actors of his generation.
7. John Ford
8. John Malkovich

## 4. Name a movie that you cannot watch it again after seeing how bad it was

**Score:** 288
**Body:** The Flash is terrible, I don’t why they approved this.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The last airbender 2010
  - I saw it on opening night, packed house, most people wearing Last Airbender cosplay.  It was the only time I saw a room full of fans stand up and boo the credits of a movie
2. Rise of Skywalker
  - It’s impressive how little I have an urge to rewatch that movie compared to every other film in the saga.
  - After how bad The Last Jedi was, I gave zero fucks about Rise Of Skywalker. And it still managed to be worse than I expected. I don’t even remember the plot.

Disney should go out of business after destroying the biggest franchise in history.
3. Joker 2
  - Was it that bad? I liked the first one but haven’t been motivated to watch the second.
4. Ice cube's war of the worlds
5. Madame Web
  - The first movie in thirty five years I actually stood up to walk out on, but then I realized I had nothing else to do so why not see how much worse it could get.

A lot.

The answer was it got A LOT worse.

## 5. The Fall (2006) - The most beautiful film almost nobody’s seen

**Score:** 197
**Body:** Tarsem Singh made a movie that looks like $200million, but it’s really just a story about pain, imagination and connection. 

I really think every shot almost belongs in a museum. 

What’s another film that feels like a forgotten masterpiece? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Peter Jackson saw it, which got Lee Pace cast as Thranduil :)
2. Something I never see talked about when this film is discussed is how good the performance is from the little girl - she's so natural and believable, which is rare with kids in films.
  - The commentary track on the film goes into her performance a lot
3. Also has one of the most beautiful and intriguing trailers.
4. I can't figure out why this is rated R. It is about adult themes, but it was told in a way in which you might tell a child about such things. It is a good movie.

A movie that I have not seen in a while, so maybe I am misremembering, that I thought was a brilliant, maybe not a masterpiece, was The Messenger: The Story of Joan of Arc. And also Oh Brother, Where Art Thou? and Raising Arizona.
  - I didn't even know it was rated R despite seeing it a dozen times. Probably the focus on suicide
5. I love how half the movies posted on here are always ones that “no one’s ever seen”
6. I agree. This is easily on my list of favorite movies.
7. I see The Fall mentioned regularly when the conversation is about cinematography.
8. Top 5 for me

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Just watched Whatever (1999), really enjoyed the protagonist's inner monologue + third party narrator through out the movie. What other movies have very effective narration that adds greatly to the movie?

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Barry Lyndon had some sparse but very effective and pleasant narration that really sold the whole package.

What are other great movies that use narration heavily and well?

The Shawshank Redemption

  
Forrest Gump

  
Fight Club

  
Apocalypse Now

  
Any other good ones? I know that a lot of modern cinema thought thinks of narration as as simple and cheap trick (show don't tell) but all those movies greatly benefitted from it.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love Casino’s use of multiple voice over. It’s almost like a documentary interviews interwoven throughout the film.

Characters for the limit Characters for the limit Characters for the limit
2. A ton of Martin Scorsese's movies do this. Goodfellas, Casino, Taxi Driver, Wolf of Wall Street, and a few others. 

If you haven't seen much of his filmography, I extremely recommend it. His movies are very watchable and enjoyable.
3. Having recently rewatched it since its theatrical release, I found the narrative of The Departed to be a model of efficiency, both in form and content. The Freudian sexual subtext completely escaped me on my first viewing. And I ended up thinking it was the best-constructed film of his career, a true genre film that stands out in his filmography compared to the biopics and chronicles he usually made.
  - Weirdly I started The Departed his morning for the first time in about 15 years (time flies) and noticed a few mentions of Freud in the script in the first 20 mins or so. But can you explain what you mean about the sexual subtext? I haven't picked up on that. 
    - Without going into too much detail, the film explores the surrogate fathers of the two main characters, the repressed sexuality of Matt Damon's character, and the themes of inheritance, fatherhood, and procreation. Ultimately, it's a mythological thriller, featuring characters from classic crime dramas elevated to the status of gods and demigods, all clashing in conflict. It's truly a fascinating and unique film when you delve a little deeper. I just rewatched it, and I'll probably write a post about it once I've had a chance to fully absorb all these themes.
      - And now that I think about it, he made \*The Aviator\* before this film, then \*Shutter Island\*. Those were also films about mental health and psychology. Since Scorsese is an auteur, I feel like he had a period focused on these themes. In my opinion, it was an underrated period compared to his earlier work. More recently, he's been tackling aging and death. He's a fascinating filmmaker; all of this deserves to be analyzed in detail.
        - Thanks for these posts, I agree, really interesting stuff. I recently watched the Mr Scorsese docu series (terrific) and he says in that how he's always exploring the spiritual, seeing the explored themes collected within eras is fascinating.
4. Blast of Silence (1961) has one of the most effective uses of narration in film. True, it’s a little overblown but I think it works. It’s central to the film as a whole:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blast_of_Silence
5. My favorite overlooked film is [Stanley Kubrick’s The Killing (1956)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Killing_(film) ), his first (or maybe second) full-length film. It features one of the most prolific over-voice actors of the time, Art Gilmore, known for his work on the TV series Highway Patrol.
6. The Thin Red Line and A Hidden Life. Definitely not Blade Runner. Goodfellas is really good. I know a lot of people hate it, but I like Ad Astra. The Assassination of Jesse James By the Coward Robert Ford uses lines from the book as voice over, and it works really well; The Age of Innocence does the same thing.

## 2. A House of Dynamite (2025) is 1/3 of a good movie

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I watched "A House of Dynamite" yesterday and, unfortunately, I have to report my disappointment.

\[I'm not a movie critic, I don't know how to write movie critique literature, and I have to admit that a majority of movie critique literature I read goes over my head. What I try to focus on is maybe being more disciplined than many professional movie critics, in anchor my opinions to objective standards. I'll do my best at keeping my more subjective opinions out of this write-up.\]

SPOILER ALERT - I'll mention plot developments that will ruin the movie for you if you haven't seen it yet. Watch it. It's still an enjoyable movie even if you concur with it having the flaws I lament. And if you disagree, it's an even more enjoyable one.

Premise: I'm a lover of the nuclear crisis movie genre. I've seen them all.

I think it's impossible watch this movie without measuring it against the many (and many great ones) that have been made on the topic: The Day After, Fail Safe (both the 1964 original, and its 2000 remake), War Games, By Dawn's Early Light, Crimson Tide. Bigelow has chosen a genre in which a lot of good work has already been done. The comparison is hard.

In the first thirty minutes of the movie we learn that an SLBM has been launched toward the US, and later we learn it's targeting Chicago. Our response forces send up two interceptor missiles which fail, and the device is set to hit Chicago. The exposition is extremely procedural: we literally sit in the rooms where decisions are taken, with the frustration of having to make decisions potentially affecting hundreds of million of lives, but on the basis of limited and ambiguous information. I'm no expert, but the procedural aspects seem well researched and re-created. The cast is stellar. The acting is top-notch. The dialogues are reasonably realistic. There doesn't seem to be any over-the-top overdramatization that many other movies insist on.

The thing at which Bigelow excels in these first thirty minutes is the masterful creation of suspense. The build up of tension is extremely skillful and reaches all the intended intensity or more. I enjoyed that greatly. The device is now only a few minutes from impact - the world is holding its breath. The entire horrific gamut of possible consequences from any foreseeable course of action has been explained to us. We are in the same room with people who are likely not to see the next 48 hours. We see the designated survivors being gathered and bussed to mountain complexes. We watch the moral dilemmas of them and their non-designated colleagues. We This aspect is supremely well done.

But then, hard stop. The story rewinds, and gets told again from a second perspective.

We catch a breath of relief, but now the dialogues replay, and honestly the extra level of detail offered from the second perspective is not that valuable. If it is, it's marginally valuable for character development but almost negligible to the plot line. We still don't know if Chicago is going to be incinerated. We implicitly hope that the second retelling will move a bit forward past the firs, but we'll be disappointed - it won't.

Instead, the same thing happens again. Events are told a third time. Now there's almost nothing of value we know about the plot, and the fear that we are going to be left dangling is starting to build up.

The story gets retold a third time, and then left abandoned in medias res.

We will never know whether Chicago will be incinerated. We will never know if America will choose to retaliate and how. We will never know the rest of the story. I haven't read reviews yet because I wanted to write my notes with a virgin mind, but I bet many will say "that's the entire point". In other words, critics will say it was emphatically the writer's and the director's intent to leave us with a very partial view of the story precisely to signify that, in a nuclear conflict, the Fog of War dominates, and partiality of information is THE dominating factor. In a full-out nuclear conflict, most of us will die without exactly knowing the sequence of events. In fact, not even our political and military leaders might know exactly what happened. It's fair to give the viewer a taste of what that feels like. And I'm ok with that. I'll take it. And still it feels like a lazy excuse for the lazy storyline.

What I dislike is the initial, deceiving fast event progression, getting the viewer to 1/3 of the run time with a built up expectations we will see twice more as many events toward a resolution, but then abruptly being denied ANY more events. We will rather be strung along for the remaining two thirds of the movie with no more plot development.

So, while the overall production quality is high, I find that the story development choices can't, IMHO, but leave the viewer profoundly disappointed. That's why my feeling is that the movie is "a third" of a very good movie, repeated three times, rather than a good movie.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree with what you’ve said, and the first couple of comments I’ve read as well (both supportive). To me it felt more like the first few episodes of a Netflix Original TV series than a movie. I expected a lot more from Bigelow and especially Elba, who breezes in and out like a man collecting a paycheque while giving zero hoots.
2. I am so frickin tired of: dun. dun. DUN. DUNNNN. *rewiiiiind* now it all plays again but from someone else’s perspective! 

It is such a lame, lazy, boring way to tell a story. It’s no inventive nor is it captivating. It’s how you stretch 30min of story into 90. 

And the whole less is more thing is also a copout. Show me something.
3. Yeah, it sucked. Especially the last third with the president. Didn't buy Elba as the Prez for a second and the entire sequence lacked tension. Agree too with the other commenter that giving each character the barest backstory was totally ineffectual (who cares if random character we'll see for one scene is engaged or whatever). Really disappointing, especially from the director who made Hurt Locker.
4. I have to agree with you. While I appreciated the escalating tension and the look behind the curtains for the first 1/3, I didn’t think the remainder of the movie added anything at all. Also agree with another commenter that the more personal elements of the characters did not add anything of value. While I understand that everyone in the decision and action chain are people, their human flaws under pressure only made me think of their selfishness and incompetence, and their ultimate unsuitability for their roles and responsibilities. And seeing just how many overly fat asses in khakis are getting saved in a bunker doesn’t signal much hope for the future either.
5. I agree the 1st 3rd is compelling and the last 2 3rd's are just frustrating. Even more so because the beginning of the movie is so good and gets you so invested in the outcome. In the end I felt the writer just didn't have the courage to tell the full story, and the final segments are just mediocre.
6. I guess I’m the only one who enjoyed the whole thing. It was clear to me not too long into it how it was going to end. So once I realized that, the way everything played out was riveting and realistic enough for me…
7. I have basically the same thoughts as well. Another thing to add - I gave zero fucks about the personal elements of the story line (the security deputy’s pregnant wife, the guy’s daughter in Chicago, and Ferguson’s husband and child). It was all half baked and lazy.

I saw the movie with some friends and they ate it all up and thought everything was superb from a story telling perspective.
8. I can only assume that Bigelow was simply trying to convey the impact of the situation on all the people involved as a way of showing the human being instead of government workers. Some examples being the guy who was gonna propose to his gf, the secretary's strained relationship with his daughter, the officer who had an argument over the phone with someone in his personal life, etc. The problem is that it wasn't necessary at all. The majority who have seen it will tell you that those first 30 minutes made everything very clear in terms of understanding the gravity of the situation. The high possibility of nuclear detonation/war in a story does that very easily. 

What ends up happening is that as soon as the second part starts, you know what you're in for and it takes all the momentum away. The only thing to look forward to is the ending, which doesn't really happen. It'd be fine if the final 2/3 were done differently but the combination is what annoyed a lot of people. I'd be curious to hear how she came to the decision for how the movie unfolded.
9. Let’s be honest with each other. *One* of the reasons we watch these movies is we wanna see the dynamite go boom. Yeah the Day After is somber and all but you can’t pretend you weren’t entertained by the 20 minute montage of corn farmers getting turned to dust. Our morbid curiosity as humans has us programmed to be entertained by stuff like that. If Threads ended right before things got bad then you, and I, wouldn’t like it. 

A House of Dynamite doesn’t take our morbid curiosity into account. It’s like it says “oh we’re gonna spare you the carnage you secretly want to see, here’s the first act three times.”
10. Hard disagree. There is no way to capture the gamut of involvement and decisionmaking needed in an 18 min response window real-time without retelling the same event from multiple perspectives.

I get most people found the first 1/3 the most compelling, as they were most sensational, but for me more long-term compelling were the later acts showing how experts are or aren't integrated into critical conversations, the nebulousness of diplomatic communications, the muck of interagency decisionmaking, and of course the nuclear briefcase decision flow.

Like IRL, there isn't a whole cinematic hour plus to luxuriously deliberate intense thoughts. You 100% are in the position of holding your post or calling your loved ones. 

I think the last act is definitely the weakest one, but in some sense, it's also the most realistic one because the President is just a civilian doing hypefest campaign things most of his day and then gets 15min to make an insane decision with the bare amount of context that's managed to trickled thru to them from all the other bureaucratic bullshit. For me, that's the most terrifying thing, a 4th wall breaking terror that's bigger than the film itself.

## 3. Zombie Flesh Eaters and its dissection of the impacts of colonialism

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Zombie Flesh Eaters, or Zombi 2 as its called in most of the world, is a 1979 horror film, and I usually end up rewatching it once a month or so, and I have for about 6 years. It’s a movie I know inside and out, and every time I rewatch it I always wonder why its seen simply as some low brow splatter grindhouse film when its genuinely an incredible look at colonialism and its impacts on America. Many people have pointed it out and done deep dives on it, but it still seems like critics don’t get it. I can understand the point of view that its just a splatter movie. It moves at a snails fucking pace, drawing out the nasty scenes to an unrealistic and absurd degree. The gore stays on camera forever and writer/director Lucio Fulci really wants you to soak the dread and fucking dreariness in. Beneath that, right away we are given a clue as to what the movie REALLY means. The characters are arrogant pretentious people who look down upon other cultures. The doctor character, who gets the most screentime outside of the main two, is explained right away as the kind of white person who goes to the Carribean to “solve” what he see as issues, only making them 10x worse. The characters are not portrayed as evil or as constantly full of hatred, they only speak of their opinions on these things in passing. The fact that they view other cultures and religions lowly isnt something that occupies their minds, it really drives home this portrayal of disdain for it. The New York scenes have these wide open shots ment to be seen as beautiful, but when the scenes focus in its only ever to show nastiness. On the Caribbean island, the wide shots are accompanied by characters calling the place underdeveloped and third world, but once focused in, it shows this deep culture built by hand with nothing but a sense of pureness. It just drives home how “progress” isn’t what americans are talking about when they view other countries they see as lower. Its the fact that these other places have culture. These places and cultures were not crafted at the expense of others or with an arrogant self-righteousness and built on blood. One of my favorite scenes in the entire movie is when the corpses of the spanish conquistadors rise from the dead, and, as I thought since my first few viewings and I saw many thought the same, it shows that these arrogant people who’ve come to another place at the expense of its people, are now the victims of the original violence that gave them this mindset. On the surface, yeah, the scene has some of the best zombie design of all time, a great soundtrack, and creative camerawork, so it’s easy to get distracted, but its a keystone scene accentuating this theme of the cycle of historical violence and how it continued even after perpetrators have long passed. They pass down their ideas of arrogance and self righteousness for centuries and those ideas become the status quo. They become the socially normal mindset. All of that, on top of the genius commentary and filmmaking, sprinkled on is just  genuinely enjoyable entertainment. It’s got amazing gore effects, jumpscares, a scene of a fucking zombie actor fistfighting a REAL SHARK underwater. I think its incredible that you can have fun with all the things going on, but at the same time, there’s so much room to dig deep into the meanings and other things going on behind the scenes. Fulci said it himself in an interview shortly before he passed. It doesn’t matter what mood you’re in, it’s a film for both lowbrow horror enjoyers and fans of intelligent, deep cinema, two groups who rarely interact otherwise.

## 4. How The 400 Blows and François Truffaut Redefined Filmmaking

**Score:** 8
**Body:** Today, the definition of the term “Auteur” varies from person to person, but when the term was first coined in the pages of Cahiers du Cinéma in the 1950s by then-film critic François Truffaut, it had a more precise meaning. He believed that the director was not just a simple craftsperson but rather a serious artist who used the language of cinema to express their worldview. Shortly thereafter, Truffaut would test his cinematic hypothesis and direct his first film, *The 400 Blows*. Upon release*,* the film helped kick off the French New Wave and, more significantly, the European Art Cinema movement, which inspired a worldwide revolution in film production and evaluation. [To trace the origins of the term auteur and Truffaut’s impact on film history, I made a video essay exploring the production of The 400 Blows. ](https://youtu.be/4uoTsBJ4SDE)

As a child and young adult in Paris during the 1940s, Truffaut absorbed a plethora of Golden Age Hollywood films that, for commercial reasons, were made in the classical style, in which form was deemphasized and intended to be invisible so as not to draw attention away from the film’s story.  Because of the dehumanizing, factory-like nature of this system, Hollywood filmmakers had few opportunities to experiment and personally express themselves artistically in their work. In a vast majority of cases, even the director was relegated to rendering an impersonal, objective reality and simply staged action in front of the camera. That said, during the Golden Age, a small legion of directors existed, most of whom Truffaut identified and valorized in his film criticism, such as John Ford, Howard Hawks, and Alfred Hitchcock, who defied the commercial constraints placed upon them. By leveraging the system’s resources and coalescing their collaborators’ efforts, these directors used their unique position within the industrial framework of the Hollywood Studio System production model to author a film with a distinctly personal, singular, subjective vantage point. With this context, it becomes understood that Truffaut’s goal in writing about film was not simply to elevate the role of the director wholesale and diminish the contributions of the cast and crew, but instead to argue how the working conditions present during the Golden Age of Hollywood generated a production environment that awarded the director, above all others, the creative latitude needed to author a film. 

That said, Though Truffaut’s criticism was rooted in unraveling the complex machinery that produced Hollywood films, as an independent filmmaker in France, when it came time to to direct his first feature length film, *The 400 Blows*, he did not have access to anywhere near the degree of resources that directors in the studio system had, forcing him to devise a different approach to construct his film. As a result, drawing on his own unstable childhood, he made *The 400 Blows* a personal, honest coming-of-age story that dispenses with the stiffness of studio filmmaking. By using newly invented, lightweight, and compact handheld cameras that did not require the massive amounts of light available only on soundstages, Truffaut shot *The 400 Blows* on real locations throughout Paris and cast unknown actors to create a naturalistic, quasi-documentary atmosphere. After years of theoretically writing about how directors can leverage their position within the film production pipeline to make a film that reflects their personal vantage point, Truffaut had done so himself.

Among film historians, the release of *The 400 Blows* marks the emergence of “The French New Wave,” an artistic movement defined by its rejection of traditional Hollywood techniques in favor of experimenting with new stylistic tricks such as hand-held cinematography, editing featuring jump-cuts, and characters who directly addressed the audience to explore relevant existential social themes. With the assistance of other French filmmakers like Agnes Varda, Jacques Demy, and Jean-Luc Godard, the French New Wave contributed to a movement that would define cinema in Europe during the post-war period: European Art Cinema.

Acting as an antithesis to commercially minded Hollywood films, European art cinema simultaneously reimagined and rejected the rules and techniques that defined classic Hollywood by purposefully embracing their limitations to redefine how films convey meaning. By breaking standard filmmaking practices, these films championed individuality over formalism, resulting in director-driven art pieces rather than pure entertainment reliant on spectacle. Across Europe during the 1960s, in countries ranging from Italy, Sweden, Spain, Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Yugoslavia, a new generation of filmmakers inspired by the French New Wave looked to break down the artificial elements of filmmaking employed by Hollywood to reveal a truth about the region of the world they were from. 



[This summary is just a brief recap of the research I did, and I encourage you to watch my full video if this subject interests you further. ](https://youtu.be/4uoTsBJ4SDE)Regardless, I welcome and look forward to any discussion this post elicits.

## 5. 2nd viewing of Sirāt - a few thoughts

**Score:** 5
**Body:** **Contains spoilers**

I returned to the world of Sirāt last night, wanting to enjoy seeing it in the cinema one last time before it was no longer showing in my village. In the opinions I heard as I left the cinema, there were many complaints about the film's lack of substance, saying it was nothing more than a long, violent and well-filmed music video. This brought me back to my own observations during my first viewing. But I felt that the film still had something to say and that you had to scratch beneath the surface of its thunderous soundtrack and the few traumatic sequences in the second half to find a kind of truth and an allegory of a world on the brink of collapse.

First observation (for what it's worth, I don't claim to be saying anything intelligent) about the sound and more specifically the Kangding Ray score. Not only is it omnipresent, it is also very loud. If we consider that a film is made up of several layers of elements superimposed on top of each other, it is often the case that the music is not in the foreground, so as to accompany the action rather than overshadow it. In Sirāt, Oliver Laxe decides to give music centre stage, giving it such importance that it takes precedence over every other aspect of the film. But the director does not do this just for the pleasure of blasting techno music into the audience's ears and numbing our senses in the process. Right from the opening scenes of the film, the music is produced by the ravers. While the film's sound mixing makes it the dominant element, it is also diegetic. The music we hear is the music that the characters hear and produce. The film opens with this superimposition of techno and monumental landscapes, giving Kangding Ray's music a mystical, religious aspect from the outset, which is then joined by the rave ritual. The ravers are believers and music is their god.

This religious aspect of music is underlined, among other things, in the brief moment when Stef (Stefania Gadda) enters the small shack where the television is still on, showing images of worshippers walking around the black stone of the Kaaba in Mecca. A new track begins at this point, in which a voice recites an excerpt from the surah Maryam. This surah has two distinctive features that make it unique in the Quran. Firstly, it is ‘the only surah named after a female figure, and indeed the only woman whose name is mentioned in the Quran’ (source: Wikipedia). Secondly, it tells the story of Mary (Maryam), the mother of Jesus. This makes it a text that some consider to be ‘a link between the three great monotheistic religions: Islam, Judaism and Christianity’ (source: Wikipedia). Music and trance as bridges between souls, as links between people, music that overcomes differences and allows us to commune in the ecstasy of the rave. And during the final escape, when the survivors abandon their vehicles and the bodies of their friends, Laxe, who is also a Muslim convert, decides to film one last time the loudspeakers that have brought them together on numerous occasions and will have carried them to their deaths.

The second observation is absence and fatality. First, the absence of speech. At the beginning of the film, the music drowns out everything else, making speech impossible to hear. Then we discover this father, accompanied by his son, who is searching for his daughter, who has been missing for months and may have disappeared. Once the small group is on its way to a hypothetical rave in the depths of the Moroccan desert, the spectre of war makes itself known on the radio, which is also completely absent from the screen, except for a parade of military vehicles whose purpose and destination we will never know. And finally, there is the desert. A place of absence and deprivation. Absence of life, water, vegetation, humanity, security. It is by stripping his film down, by reducing its content to the essentials – the music, the road, the stages, the breaks to rest and eat – that he touches on the heart of his story, where arbitrary destiny and fatality come into play. Esteban's brutal death is also that of all the children ravaged by war, taken away from their powerless parents without trial. In the same way, the minefield will take away the unfortunate ones who ventured where they should not have, killed arbitrarily for practising their ‘religion’. The deaths in Sirāt are the deaths of any war and its blind, definitive strikes. And the survivors are condemned to wander in limbo, embarked here on a train moving through an endless landscape with no visible destination. And so, rather than seeking a limited (and inevitably simplistic) interpretation of a specific geopolitical event, Laxe's film aims for universality, both in its most beautiful aspects, those of community, communion through music, sharing and mutual aid, and in its most terrible moments, when misfortune blindly tears bodies from the world of the living.

This review is a translation of my original text in french, [posted on letterboxd](https://boxd.it/bxcQ7n). The translation was made with the help of Deepl.
